summary: I grew up in Springfield, Ohio, and when I was drafted, I didn't have much choice but to go into the military. It was a small town, and the options back then were pretty limited. Leaving Springfield was tough, but I knew joining the Marine Corps was the best path for me.

I was actually drafted and had the option to join either the United States Army or the Marine Corps. I ended up choosing the Marine Corps because I felt their training was superior. Later, when I got malaria, I was treated at an Army hospital, which was a memorable experience with movies and USO shows.

I chose the United States Marine Corps because I believed they gave the best training. Basic training was brutal but transformative; it really turned boys into men. The Marine Corps is a unique fraternity, and even my son later joined because of that bond. I served as a rifleman during the Vietnam War and experienced the tough conditions firsthand.

When we first arrived for boot camp in 1969, we couldn't fly into San Diego because of fog, so we were diverted to Los Angeles. After a rough night of standing on yellow footprints, we finally got to San Diego around 3:30 in the morning. It was the start of my Marine Corps training journey.

Our plane was diverted to Los Angeles because San Diego was fogged in back in 1969. We landed there around 1:30 in the morning, and it was a ghost town compared to what we expected. A drill instructor was there to meet us, and he immediately started yelling and enforcing discipline as we were loaded onto a bus to head to San Diego.

I served as a rifleman in the Vietnam War, arriving in Da Nang in the pouring rain without a rifle initially. We spent most of our time out in the bush rather than at rear bases like An Hoa or Hill 65. The conditions were tough with rain, mosquitoes, and constant stress, but we adapted and persevered through it all.

An Hoa was where our regimental rear was located during the Vietnam War, but we rarely spent time there. It was considered a rear area with better facilities like cots and a chow hall, but infantrymen like us preferred staying out in the bush. Going back there sometimes felt like being treated as second-class citizens.

Hill 65 was the battalion rear area during my time in Vietnam, but like An Hoa, we didn't spend much time there. It had better sleeping arrangements and food than the bush, but infantry guys often felt looked down upon when sent back. Most of my time was spent out in the field rather than at places like Hill 65.

I flew into Da Nang on a commercial airline during the Vietnam War, which surprised me. It was raining heavily when we landed, and I didn't even have a rifle yet despite training for months. Da Nang was a relatively secure area back then, but it was still a shock to arrive in such a different world.

My son served in the Marine Corps and went to Iraq twice during the Iraq War. I tried to talk him out of it, suggesting he find somewhere to learn something else, but he was determined to be a Marine. His experiences in Iraq made me proud of the Marine Corps fraternity and the values it instills.

My dad served in World War II, and he carried a Bible with a brass shield that his mother sent him. He often talked about the terrible conditions, like the snow and cold. Hearing his stories gave me perspective on the hardships of war and the sacrifices made by those before me.

During my Vietnam tour, I got to meet my wife in Hawaii for a week of R&R, which was a great experience. It was a welcome break from the hardships of the war and a chance to reconnect with loved ones. Hawaii felt like a peaceful contrast to the combat zones I was used to.

I was awarded the National Defense Service Medal during my military service. It was one of the standard medals given for serving during a time of conflict like the Vietnam War. While it wasn't as prestigious as some others, it still represented my commitment and service.

I received the Combat Action Ribbon for my time in Vietnam as a rifleman. It recognized that I was actively engaged in combat situations during my deployment. That ribbon reminds me of the tough experiences and the dangers we faced in the field.

I did not receive the Bronze Star Medal during my service, but I was aware of its significance. It was awarded for acts of heroism or meritorious service, and while I earned some ribbons, the Bronze Star was not among them. Still, I respected those who earned it.

While I was hospitalized with malaria during the Vietnam War, the United Service Organizations (USO) put on shows every three days. Those USO shows were a memorable morale boost during a tough time. They helped us feel connected to home and lifted our spirits.
